title: Anno Pax Romana
subtitle: A self-initiated redesign exploring how one small addition can remove far more friction than it creates.

intro.heading: One extra click that removed twenty
intro.body:
I love strategy games, but I play them on console. That combination is rare, and it creates a specific kind of friction that PC players rarely notice. Anno Pax Romana handles its console version better than most, but there is still a lot of room to improve how complex systems are navigated with a controller.

This project started from my own frustration as a player, and became a study in how one small addition can remove far more steps than it adds.

role.heading: UX/UI Designer
role.body:
This was a self-initiated redesign. I analysed the existing interface from the perspective of a console player, identified the most recurring workflow pain point, and designed a solution that addressed it without touching the rest of the UI.

I prototyped in Figma and also explored implementation directly in Unreal Engine to understand how the design would behave inside the actual game environment.

image.alt: Problem areas in the Anno Pax Romana route editor interface

problem.heading: A fragmented workflow
problem.body:
The most recurring problem for me was managing trade routes. Warnings would appear constantly, and every time I needed to investigate, I had to jump in and out of menus, wait for loading, and lose my place in whatever I was doing.

As a UX designer I think in user flows, how many steps does it take to complete an action? The trade route workflow had too many. And the more I mapped it, the clearer it became that the problem was not any single menu, it was that the right information was never in the same place as the decision that needed it.
bullets: Ship cargo cannot be viewed inside the route editor
Island stock levels are not visible when configuring routes
Warning icons lack clear explanations for route problems
Console navigation requires multiple menu transitions

approach.heading: A solution that works for everyone
approach.body:
What surprised me was that I did not need to redesign the existing UI at all. The interface works well and shares visual language between PC and console, which is important for recognition.

What was missing was a single addition. One extra click to open a panel that removed twenty clicks of navigating in and out of menus. I had found a solution that worked equally well on both platforms, not just a console workaround but a genuine improvement for everyone.

cards.title: UI Audit
cards.body: Documented the route editor interface and identified missing information needed for decision-making.

cards.title: Workflow Mapping
cards.body: Mapped how players diagnose trade route issues and counted exactly how many steps each action required.

cards.title: Rapid Prototyping
cards.body: Iterated on interface layouts in Figma to test how additional information could be integrated without clutter.

outcome.heading: Route Assistant Panel (RAP)
outcome.body: The redesign introduces a collapsible side panel inside the Route Editor called the Route Assistant Panel (RAP). The panel surfaces key logistics information without forcing players to navigate away from the editor.
bullets: Ship cargo overview visible directly in the Route Editor
Island stock levels integrated into the route configuration screen
Warning and diagnostic panel, ability to quickly identify and address route problems
Controller shortcuts designed for fast console navigation

image.alt: The Route Assistant Panel in the Anno Pax Romana route editor

unrealPrototyping.heading: Prototyping UI directly in Unreal Engine
unrealPrototyping.body:
Instead of only designing interfaces in static mockups, I also explored how the UI could work directly inside Unreal Engine.

Using UMG widgets and Blueprint structures, I created small interactive prototypes to test layout, information hierarchy, and interaction behaviour in the actual game environment.

This allowed me to experiment with how UI elements respond to gameplay systems and helped bridge the gap between design ideas and implementation.

images.alt: A simple UI style guide used as a reference when building widgets in Unreal.
Early UI layout prototype built in Unreal using UMG widgets.
Blueprint structure connecting UI elements and item data for quick testing inside the engine.

keyLearning.heading: The order of things matters
keyLearning.body:
The Unreal prototyping taught me something I did not expect: trying to build a full design system in the engine took too long and pulled focus away from the actual interaction. I should have prioritised getting the core mechanic and game flow working first, so I could iterate on what actually mattered.

The lesson was not that implementation is unimportant. It is that the order matters.

cards.title: Workflow first
cards.body: Understanding how players move through the interface, especially with console input, helped determine what information should appear first and what could stay hidden.

cards.title: Don't redesign what works
cards.body: The existing UI was good. Recognising that early meant I could focus on what was actually missing rather than rebuilding something that already functioned.
